1: Chapter 27 is a legal appendix to the Holiness Code, addressing the commutation of dedicated persons and objects. YHWH speaks to Moses with the familiar introductory formula.
2: ish ki yafle neder beerekcha nefashot l-YHWH: when a person makes a special vow (yafle neder) involving persons at your valuation (beerekcha) to YHWH. Pala (in the hiphil: to make extraordinary, to set apart by vow) — a special/extraordinary vow as distinct from regular offerings. Erekh (valuation, assessed value) is the central term of Leviticus 27: the monetary value assigned to persons or things dedicated to YHWH by vow. The erekh system provides a mechanism for commuting the dedication of persons into money payments, allowing the person to continue normal life while fulfilling the vow financially.
3: vehayah erkecha hazachar miben esrim shanah vead ben-shishim shanah: the valuation for a male from 20 to 60 years old shall be 50 shekels of silver. The erekh schedule by age and gender: males 20-60 = 50 shekels. The scale reflects productive economic capacity — the peak working years carry the highest valuation. This is not a measure of human worth before God but a practical financial schedule for commuting vows in a subsistence economy.
4: ve-im neqevah hi vehayah erkecha sheloshim shoqel: if female, the valuation shall be 30 shekels. The female valuation (30 shekels, 20-60 years) reflects the economic productivity differential in an agricultural society. This is an economic assessment, not a theological statement about the equal dignity of women before God (cf. Galatians 3:28 for the new covenant's transformation).
5: ve-im miben-chamesh shanim vead ben-esrim shanah: from 5 to 20 years: male 20 shekels, female 10 shekels. The reduced valuations for younger persons reflect lower economic productivity. The age-brackets (birth-1 month, 1-5 years, 5-20, 20-60, 60+) provide a comprehensive schedule covering the entire life cycle.
6: ve-im miben-chodesh vead ben-chamesh shanim vehayah erkecha hazachar chamisha sheqalim kesef: from one month to five years: male 5 shekels, female 3 shekels. The inclusion of infants (from one month) in the valuation schedule shows that the vow system covers even the dedication of newborns (cf. Hannah's dedication of Samuel, 1 Samuel 1:11). The 1-month threshold corresponds to the age at which first-born sons are redeemed (Numbers 18:16), suggesting a connection between these systems.
7: ve-im miben-shishim shanah vamaalah im zachar: if 60 years and above: male 15 shekels, female 10 shekels. The elderly receive lower valuations — reduced economic productivity in old age. The entire schedule from birth through old age demonstrates that the vow system was designed to be comprehensive and practically workable rather than a burden that could not be met.
8: ve-im mak hu meerecha vehemido lifnei hakkohen veherimu hakkohen: if the person is too poor (mak = reduced, impoverished) to pay the valuation, he shall be presented before the priest, who shall assess him. Mak (from muq = to be dissolved, weakened, reduced) describes economic reduction below the ability to meet the standard valuation. The priest then sets a proportional amount based on what the person can afford — al pi asher tasig yad hannoder (according to what the hand of the vow-maker can reach). This income-proportional adjustment is a built-in equity mechanism: the vow system must not be so burdensome that poor Israelites cannot participate in worship.
9: ve-im behemah asher yaqrivu mimena qorban l-YHWH kol asher yitten mimena l-YHWH yihyeh qodesh: if the vow concerns livestock fit for offering to YHWH, everything given to YHWH shall be holy. Qorban (offering, from qarav = to bring near) — any dedicated animal becomes qodesh (holy/set apart) the moment it is designated for YHWH. The holiness is not achieved by the act of sacrifice but by the act of dedication.
10: lo yachaliyfenu velo yamir oto tov bera o ra betov veim hamir yamir beheimah beheimah vehayah-hu utemurato yihyeh qodesh: he shall not exchange or substitute (yachaliyfenu... yamir) it, good for bad or bad for good; if he should substitute one animal for another, both it and its substitute shall be holy. The anti-substitution rule protects the integrity of the dedication system: once an animal is designated for YHWH, it cannot be swapped out for a lesser animal. The attempted substitution makes both animals holy — a disincentive that discourages manipulation of the vow system.
11: ve-im kol behemah temeh asher lo yaqrivu mimena qorban l-YHWH: and if it is an unclean animal, not fit for offering to YHWH — the person shall present the animal before the priest. Unclean animals cannot be sacrificed but can still be the subject of dedication vows. The priest values them, and the person may redeem them by paying the valuation plus one-fifth (chamishito). The redemption-plus-fifth (20% surcharge) is a recurring feature of the Leviticus 27 redemption system.
12: veherimu hakkohen bein tov uvein ra: the priest shall value it between good and bad — at whatever valuation the priest sets, that it shall be. The priestly valuation authority for unclean animals is absolute: kemerkah hakkohen ken yihyeh (as the priest values it, so it shall be).
13: ve-im ga'ol yig'alenu: and if he redeems it. Ga'al (to redeem, buy back) — the key term of the Levitical redemption system. Ga'al in Leviticus 25-27 covers the redemption of land, persons, and dedicated property. The same root underlies go'el (kinsman-redeemer) — the family member with both the right and obligation to buy back what a relative has lost. The theological extension to YHWH as Israel's go'el (Isaiah 41:14; Leviticus 44:6; Leviticus 48:17) makes the redemption system a type of divine redemption.
14: ve-ish ki yaqadish et-beito qodesh l-YHWH: when a man consecrates (yaqadish) his house as holy to YHWH — the priest values it as good or bad, and at the priest's valuation it shall stand. Houses can be dedicated and then redeemed; the 20% surcharge applies here as well.
15: ve-im hamaqadish yigal et-beito veyasaf chamishit kesef erkecha alav vehayah lo: if the consecrator redeems his house, he shall add a fifth to the valuation price and it shall be his. The redemption-with-surcharge rule ensures that dedicating and immediately redeeming is not a zero-cost exercise: there is a cost to changing one's mind about a dedication. The surcharge incentivizes completion of vows and discourages treating dedication as a reversible financial instrument.
16: ve-im misedeh achuzzato yaqadish ish l-YHWH: if a person consecrates to YHWH part of his ancestral land (sedeh achuzzato). Achuzzah (possession, inherited land, from achaz = to hold, take possession) — the ancestral land of the tribe. The valuation of ancestral land is connected to the Jubilee: the value is calculated by the number of years remaining until the Jubilee. Zerah seor (seed of barley/homer) is the unit of measurement — 1 homer of barley seed = 50 shekels for a full 50-year Jubilee period.
17: im mishenat hayovel yaqadish sadahu keerkecha yaqum: if he consecrates his field from the Jubilee year, the full valuation applies. The land-valuation system is explicitly linked to the Jubilee (Lev 25): the closer to the next Jubilee, the less the land is worth for valuation purposes, since the buyer only has use of it until the Jubilee year when it reverts to the original family.
18: ve-im achar hayovel yaqadish sadahu: if after the Jubilee he consecrates his field. The priest calculates the proportional value: the remaining years until the next Jubilee determine the price. This is a time-value-of-money calculation built into the Torah — the principle that future possession is worth less than present possession.
19: ve-im ga'ol yig'al et-hasadeh hamaqadish oto: if he who consecrated the field redeems it — he shall add a fifth to the valuation price and it shall remain his. The redemption option with surcharge applies to land as to houses. The redemption right belongs to the original owner (or family member), consistent with the Jubilee's principle of preserving ancestral inheritance.
20: ve-im lo yig'al et-hasadeh ve-im-machar et-hasadeh le-ish acher: if he does not redeem the field, or if he has sold the field to another person — it cannot be redeemed anymore. The forfeiture-if-sold clause: if the consecrated land is sold to a third party before being redeemed, it permanently becomes temple property rather than reverting to the original family at Jubilee. This rule prevents using the consecration-and-sale sequence as a way to alienate ancestral land permanently.
21: vehayah hasadeh betzeto bayovel qodesh l-YHWH kesadeh hachereem: when the field reverts in the Jubilee, it shall be holy to YHWH like a devoted field (sadeh hachereem) — it becomes the priest's property. Cherem (devoted thing, irrevocable dedication) is introduced here — the category of absolute dedication that cannot be redeemed. The transition from regular consecration to cherem occurs when the normal redemption pathway is blocked.
22: ve-im et-sedeh miqnato asher lo misedeh achuzzato yaqadish l-YHWH: if he consecrates to YHWH a field that he has purchased, which is not his ancestral field. Purchased land (as opposed to ancestral inheritance) is valued differently: it belongs to the original owner at the Jubilee regardless. The valuation runs to the Jubilee year, when the field reverts to its original owner.
23: vechishuv lo hakkohen et michsat haerkcha ad shenat hayovel: the priest shall calculate for him the amount of the valuation up to the Jubilee year. The priest as financial calculator — the technical role the priesthood plays in the complex redemption-and-valuation system. The exactness of the calculation (down to specific year) reflects the systemic precision of the Jubilee framework.
24: beshenat hayovel yashov hasadeh laasher qonahu meito laasher lo achuzzat haaretz: in the Jubilee year the field shall revert to him from whom it was bought, to whom the land belongs as a possession. The Jubilee's land-restoration principle (Leviticus 25:13) applies even to consecrated purchased land. The purchased field cannot be permanently alienated from its original owner by consecration any more than by sale.
25: vechol-erkecha yihyeh besheqel hakodesh: all valuations shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary (sheqel hakodesh). Sheqel hakodesh (sanctuary/holy shekel) — a standardized unit of weight used for temple transactions, likely equivalent to 20 gerahs. The specification of the sanctuary shekel prevents monetary manipulation through use of lighter civil shekels. The sanctuary maintains its own weight standard — a form of financial integrity built into the system.
26: ach bechor asher yevukkar l-YHWH behemah lo yaqadish ish oto: but a firstborn of animals, which belongs to YHWH as a firstborn, no one may dedicate. Bechor (firstborn) animals already belong to YHWH by right of firstborn (Exodus 13:2; Numbers 18:15-17) — they cannot be further dedicated because they are already YHWH's. You cannot give what you do not own. This rule clarifies the scope of the vow/dedication system: only what belongs to the individual can be dedicated.
27: ve-im babeheimah hatemeah ufadah veerkecha veyasaf chamishito alav: if it is an unclean animal, he shall redeem it at the valuation price plus one-fifth, or if it is not redeemed, it shall be sold at the valuation. The consistent surcharge-for-redemption rule applied to unclean firstborn animals. The 20% surcharge appears seven times in the chapter — its recurrence establishes it as the chapter's primary financial principle.
28: ach kol-cherem asher yacherim ish l-YHWH mikol-asher-lo me-adam ubehemah umisedeh achuzzato lo yimmacer velo yigael kol-cherem qodesh qadashim hu l-YHWH: but nothing that a person devotes (yacherim) to YHWH as an irrevocable devotion (cherem), whether man, animal, or inherited field, shall be sold or redeemed. Every cherem is most holy (qodesh qadashim) to YHWH. Cherem (from charam = to devote irrevocably, to put under the ban) is the most absolute form of dedication in the OT — unlike the erekh-valuations of the rest of the chapter, which can be redeemed with a surcharge, cherem is irrevocable. The same word describes the ban on Canaanite cities (Joshua 6:17-21) and the devoted spoil of war. Its irrevocability means that cherem-dedicated persons face death (v29) — the person cannot be redeemed because they have been absolutely given over to divine disposal.
29: kol-cherem asher yucherem min-haadam lo yipadeh mot yumat: no devoted person (cherem min-haadam) who is irrevocably devoted shall be redeemed — he shall surely be put to death. The cherem-death penalty applies to human cherem-dedications. This verse has been interpreted variously: as referring to criminals condemned by the community and devoted to God's judgment; as referring to enemy warriors under the ban; or as the logical extreme of the cherem principle where absolute devotion to YHWH means the person is no longer available for any earthly use. The NT transformation of cherem-logic: Paul's anathema (from ana-thema = devotion/curse) in Galatians 1:8-9 invokes the same absolute-devotion language, applied to those who pervert the gospel.
30: vechol-maaser haaretz mizera haaretz mipri haaetz l-YHWH hu qodesh l-YHWH: all the tithe of the land — whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees — is YHWH's; it is holy to YHWH. Maaser (tithe, tenth, from eser = ten) — the tenth portion that belongs to YHWH by right of his ownership of the land (Psalms 24:1). The tithe is declared qodesh l-YHWH (holy to YHWH) — set apart, belonging to God's domain. The tithe is not an act of generosity but a recognition of ownership: the land is YHWH's, and the tithe acknowledges this. Malachi 3:10 builds on this: robbing YHWH of the tithe is robbing him of what is already his.
31: ve-im ga'ol yig'al ish mimaaasero chamishito yosif alav: if a person redeems any of his tithe, he shall add a fifth to it. The redemption-plus-fifth rule applies to tithes as well as dedicated property: if the tithe-payer wishes to keep the actual grain or fruit and pay money instead, the 20% surcharge applies. The surcharge makes cash-commutation slightly more expensive than actual tithe-payment, preserving the integrity of the tithing system.
32: vechol-maaser baqar vatzon kol asher-yaavor tachat hashavet haasiri yihyeh-qodesh l-YHWH: and every tithe of cattle and flock, every tenth animal that passes under the staff (yaavor tachat hashavet) — the tenth shall be holy to YHWH. The animal tithe is calculated by counting: every animal passes under a staff or rod, and every tenth animal is automatically designated holy. The counting method (not human selection) ensures that the tithe cannot be manipulated by selecting inferior animals — the tenth passes over regardless of quality. This is the mechanical tithe, not subject to the vow-with-valuation system.
33: lo yevaqer bein tov lera velo yamiyrenu: he shall not select between good and bad or substitute for it (lo yamiyrenu). The non-selection, non-substitution rule for animal tithes parallels the anti-substitution rule for dedicated animals (v10). The human tendency to keep the best and give the worst is explicitly prohibited: the tenth is the tenth regardless. Ve-im hamir yamiyrenu vehayah hu utemurato yihyeh qodesh lo yigael: if he does substitute, both it and its substitute shall be holy and shall not be redeemed. The same penalty as v10: attempted manipulation results in double dedication.
34: eleh hamitzvot asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe el-benei Yisrael behar Sinai: these are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai. The closing formula of the entire book of Leviticus — Sinai as the authoritative source of all the laws in the book, including the legal appendix of Leviticus 27. The Sinai-authority formula brackets Leviticus as a unified corpus of divine instruction delivered at the mountain of the covenant.
